narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the hard work you've put into your undergrad and LSAT prep—it's tough to hear that your application to law school didn't make the cut based on our predictive model for bar exam success. The model looked at your profile and estimated a 61% chance of not passing the bar, which is why they had to pass on your application this time. What stood out most was your LSAT score of 27, which was the biggest signal pulling toward that tougher outlook, as it's below the levels that typically light the path to bar success for students like you. Layered on top of that, your Year 3 decile of 2.0 showed some real challenges in keeping up with the pack academically as things got more demanding, making the model worry about the endurance needed for the bar grind. Your Year 1 decile of 3.0 echoed that early pattern, suggesting those hurdles might stick around in a rigorous law program. Coming from a middle-income family added another layer of concern, as the model sees that bracket sometimes stretching resources thin for the intense prep required. Even with your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.6, which is a bright spot, it wasn't quite enough to offset those other pieces pulling in the failure direction. Your background as a white female and your plan to go full-time didn't sway things much either—they fit patterns the model associates with added risks in this prediction. It's like the model saw your story as one where early academic deciles and that LSAT dipped below the threshold, painting a picture of an uphill battle against the odds for the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential or heart—many have turned similar profiles around with targeted boosts like LSAT retakes or extra academic support. You're clearly driven, and with some focus on those top areas, you could rewrite this story next cycle. Hang in there; you've got the GPA foundation to build on.
